# Figure description: Страт_колонки_скважин_черт_49_л_6.jpg

---

## Figure Type

This is a **composite stratigraphic/lithological borehole log figure** (геологический разрез по скважинам) — specifically a set of **comparative geological cross-sections and well logs** compiled from multiple boreholes. It shows lithological columns (разрезы) drawn side-by-side with correlation lines connecting equivalent stratigraphic horizons between wells. This is a standard Soviet-era format for correlating borehole stratigraphy across a survey area.

---

## Coverage and Subject

The figure covers **multiple boreholes** drilled in the northern Kazakhstan survey area under map sheets **M-42 and N-42** (scale 1:200,000). Based on partially legible headers, the figure includes:

- A central, dominant borehole column labeled at the top — likely the **reference/key borehole** (possibly **скв. Ф-42** or similar designation — partially illegible)
- At least **4–6 additional borehole columns** arranged to the left and right of the central column, including what appears to be columns labeled with designations such as **скв. М-40-9**, **скв. М-40-7**, and others (partially legible)
- Titles above individual columns reference **геологический разрез по скважине** (geological cross-section through borehole) for several named boreholes
- Depth ranges appear to extend from surface to approximately **800–1,200+ metres** based on the scale gradations visible on the columns
- Geographic references include what appear to be **river valley** (р. Бурла or similar — [illegible]) and references to **Новосибирской** or similar regional geological survey jurisdiction

---

## Key Content

### Column Structure
Each borehole column is drawn vertically with:
- A **lithological pattern fill** indicating rock type (hatching, dotting, brick patterns, wavy lines) — standard Soviet geological symbology:
  - **Dotted/granular pattern** = sandstone or sand
  - **Brick/block pattern** = limestone or consolidated carbonate rock
  - **Wavy/horizontal lines** = clay, shale, or mudstone
  - **Cross-hatching** = crystalline or igneous basement
  - **Fine dots** = aleurolite (siltstone) or fine-grained clastic
- **Depth tick marks** at regular intervals along the left margin of each column
- **Formation/horizon labels** written beside the columns in Cyrillic, indicating stratigraphic unit designations (e.g., **P₂**, **K₁**, **J**, **T**, **Pz** — Paleogene, Cretaceous, Jurassic, Triassic, Paleozoic — though specific values are partially illegible at this scan resolution)

### Correlation Lines
Horizontal **correlation lines** connect equivalent stratigraphic boundaries between borehole columns, allowing the reader to trace:
- The **top and base of individual sedimentary sequences**
- **Unconformities** or erosional surfaces
- **Aquifer or reservoir horizons** (marked as "горизонт" — horizon, with numbered subdivisions visible, e.g., "Горизонт 6 подгоризонта", "Горизонт 5 подгоризонта", "Горизонт 4 подгоризонта", "Горизонт 3 подгоризонта", "Горизонт 2 подгоризонта", "Горизонт 1 подгоризонта")

### Stratigraphic Subdivisions
The right-center column (likely the reference section) shows **numbered sub-horizons** (подгоризонты) — at least **6 sub-horizons** are labeled, suggesting subdivision of a key aquifer or mineralized formation into discrete productive intervals. These sub-horizon labels are a notable feature, suggesting **hydrogeological or mineral resource evaluation** purposes.

### Depth Scale
The vertical scale appears to be approximately **1:500 to 1:1000** (Soviet standard for borehole logs at this scale). Depth values visible along column margins suggest the sections extend to **600–1,200 m** depth, with the deepest sections in the central columns.

### Lithological Observations
- The upper portions of most columns show **alternating clastic sequences** (sands, clays, siltstones) consistent with **Mesozoic–Cenozoic cover**
- Mid-sections show more **consolidated/carbonate lithologies**
- The basal portions of the deepest columns show **crystalline basement** patterns (cross-hatch or coarse texture)
- Several columns show **thin interbeds** marked distinctively — possibly coal seams, evaporites, or mineralized horizons
